description: UE5 world management: World Partition, Data Layers, Level Streaming, Level Instances, and open world architecture

entries.title: World Partition Overview
entries.description: World Partition is UE5's system for managing large open worlds. It automatically divides the world into a grid and streams cells in/out based on player distance. Replaces Level Streaming for open-world games.
entries.whatItIs: World Partition is UE5's automatic world management system that divides a single persistent level into a grid of streaming cells, loading and unloading them based on distance from streaming sources, while using One Actor Per File (OFPA) to enable multi-user collaboration and HLOD for distant content representation.
whenToUse: For open-world games with large seamless maps that cannot fit entirely in memory
When multiple developers need to edit the same world simultaneously without file conflicts (OFPA)
When you want automatic distance-based streaming without manual level boundary setup
For any project that benefits from HLOD generation for distant geometry
whenNotToUse: For small, contained levels that fit entirely in memory (single rooms, arenas, linear corridors)
When you need explicit manual control over what loads and when (use classic Level Streaming instead)
For projects targeting very low memory platforms where the streaming overhead is not justified
When your game design requires fully isolated levels with no spatial continuity (e.g., separate multiplayer maps)
commonMistakes: Placing critical gameplay actors outside the grid bounds, causing them to never stream in
Not setting up HLOD layers, leaving empty space where distant unloaded cells should show proxy geometry
Forgetting that World Partition uses One Actor Per File — source control workflows must adapt to many small files
Setting the loading range too small, causing visible pop-in as the player moves quickly
Not adding custom streaming sources for non-player cameras (spectator, security camera) that need content loaded around them
keyPoints: Enable: World Settings > World Partition > Enable World Partition
Grid-based streaming: world is divided into cells. Cells load/unload based on distance from streaming sources.
Streaming source: by default, player cameras. Can add custom streaming sources.
Loading range: configurable per grid. Actors beyond the range are unloaded from memory.
One Actor Per File (OFPA): each actor is saved as its own file, enabling multi-user editing.
HLOD (Hierarchical Level of Detail): low-detail proxies shown for distant unloaded cells.
Replaces sub-levels for open worlds. No manual level boundary setup needed.
Data Layers: categorize actors into layers that can be loaded/unloaded independently (gameplay layers, cinematic layers).

entries.title: Level Streaming (Classic)
entries.description: Level Streaming loads/unloads sub-levels at runtime. Used for indoor areas, dungeons, separate game zones, or any non-open-world map structure. Still supported alongside World Partition.
entries.whatItIs: Classic Level Streaming is UE5's original system for loading and unloading separate .umap sub-level files at runtime, triggered by volumes, Blueprint calls, or C++ code, enabling manual control over what content is in memory at any given time.
whenToUse: For indoor/outdoor transitions where you want explicit control over when areas load
For linear or semi-linear games with distinct zones (dungeons, floors, arenas)
When you need to load entirely separate gameplay environments (different game modes in different levels)
For multiplayer map transitions using Seamless Travel to avoid disconnecting clients
When memory management requires precise manual control over loaded content
whenNotToUse: For large open-world games where World Partition's automatic grid streaming is more appropriate
When you want automatic distance-based streaming without manually placing volumes or writing loading code
When multiple developers need to edit the same world space — sub-levels create file-locking conflicts
commonMistakes: Not handling the asynchronous nature of level loading — accessing actors in a level that is not yet fully loaded
Forgetting to use FLatentActionInfo correctly, causing the completion callback to never fire
Loading too many sub-levels simultaneously, causing a frame rate hitch from the loading spike
Not using SetLevelVisibility() when you want to hide a level without unloading it (cheaper than unload/reload)
Using non-Seamless Travel for multiplayer, disconnecting all clients during map transitions
keyPoints: Sub-levels: separate .umap files that load into the persistent level
Level Streaming Volumes: trigger loading when player enters a volume
Blueprint/Code: UGameplayStatics::LoadStreamLevel(), UnloadStreamLevel()
FLatentActionInfo: the callback mechanism for stream level completion
SetLevelVisibility(): show/hide a loaded level without unloading it
GetStreamingLevel(LevelName): get the ULevelStreaming object for status checks
Seamless Travel: ServerTravel for multiplayer map changes without disconnecting clients
Use for: indoor/outdoor transitions, separate game modes in different levels, memory management

entries.title: Level Instances & Packed Level Actors
entries.description: Level Instances let you use a sub-level as a reusable prefab. Place multiple instances of the same level in your world, each with independent transforms. Packed Level Actors (PLAs) convert them to lightweight, portable actors.
entries.whatItIs: Level Instances are reusable sub-level prefabs that can be placed multiple times in a world with independent transforms, while Packed Level Actors (PLAs) flatten a Level Instance into a single portable actor that integrates with World Partition and HLOD, enabling modular world-building workflows.
whenToUse: For modular building construction using repeating room templates, corridor sections, or floor plans
When you want to edit one instance of a room and have all copies update automatically
For dungeon or procedural level design using reusable tile-based pieces
Use Packed Level Actors when Level Instances need to work with World Partition streaming and HLOD
whenNotToUse: For unique one-off areas that will never be reused — just place actors directly
When the level content needs to vary significantly per instance (Level Instances share the same source)
For dynamic runtime spawning of level chunks — consider Level Streaming or runtime actor spawning instead
commonMistakes: Editing a Level Instance without realizing all instances share the same source — unintentional changes propagate everywhere
Not converting to Packed Level Actors when using World Partition, causing streaming and HLOD issues
Placing overlapping Level Instances without checking for collision or navmesh conflicts between them
Forgetting that Level Instance transforms apply to all contained actors — scale can cause unexpected results
Using Level Instances for content that needs per-instance variation — consider using child actor components or data-driven differences instead
keyPoints: Level Instance: place a sub-level multiple times in your world (like a room template)
Each instance has its own transform (position, rotation, scale)
Edit one instance and all instances update (shared source level)
Packed Level Actor: converts a Level Instance to a single actor for easier management
Packed Level Actors work with World Partition and HLOD generation
Use for: modular building pieces, repeating room layouts, dungeon tiles
Create: Content Browser > Level > place in world with Level Instance actor
